items.title: BREAKING: Since the start of 2025, US jobs numbers have now been revised down in 14 out of 17 months by a total of -710,000 jobs. May and April jobs n...
items.description_html:
BREAKING: Since the start of 2025, US jobs numbers have now been revised down in 14 out of 17 months by a total of -710,000 jobs.

May and April jobs numbers were revised down by a total of -74,000, the largest 2-month downward revision since December.

April jobs were revised down by -31,000, to +148,000, while May jobs were revised down by -43,000, to +129,000.

This means -41,765 jobs have been revised out of previously reported data, on average, in each month of this period.

If we apply this average to the +57,000 June nonfarm payrolls, it would imply just ~15,000 jobs were added last month.

Job market revisions are concerning.

items.title: Foreign investors are piling into US equities at a record pace: Cumulative year-to-date inflows from global investment funds into US equities are up t...
items.description_html:
Foreign investors are piling into US equities at a record pace:

Cumulative year-to-date inflows from global investment funds into US equities are up to ~2.5% of their total assets under management.

This percentage has more than DOUBLED since May alone.

This is also significantly above the 2002-2025 average of -0.3% in outflows for this point in the year.

By comparison, excluding the top 10% with the largest inflows and the bottom 10% with the largest outflows of the 2002-2025 period, global fund inflows averaged just ~1.5% by this point in the year.

The YTD pace has already exceeded the full-year total recorded in the middle 50% of years since 2002.

Current demand for US equities is unprecedented.

items.title: What is happening with the US labor market? The difference between nonfarm payrolls and the household survey was a massive 564,000 jobs in June. This ...
items.description_html:
What is happening with the US labor market?

The difference between nonfarm payrolls and the household survey was a massive 564,000 jobs in June.

This comes as Thursday’s job report showed +57,000 non-farm payrolls were added last month.

At the same time, -507,000 Americans lost their job, the 3rd-largest drop since January 2024, according to the household survey.

The household survey is a closely followed metric because it counts each worker only once, even if they hold multiple jobs.

Year-to-date, total employment in the household survey has declined -1.7 million, to 162.26 million, the lowest since December 2024.

Over the same period, total nonfarm employment has risen +552,000, to a record 158.98 million.

Something does not add up here.

items.title: RT ValueEquity: Windlas Biotech , reading this annual report today Capacity Utilization stands at 66% as in comparison to 59% last year , Plant 5 cert...
items.description_html:
RT ValueEquity
Windlas Biotech , reading this annual report today

Capacity Utilization stands at 66% as in comparison to 59% last year , Plant 5 certifications received and plant 6 commercial in the next 2 quarters

They are focusing on deepening wallet shares , increasing exports options and even on R&D last quarter I had asked them on call they continue to focus on customer led initiatives that help them to keep deploying incremental capital in the right way

Also one more thing I also noticed while reading is the delta of hiring in the Quality Control teams

FY25 Total : 177
FY26: 250 ( 41% increase in just 1 year for the new plants and capability ramp up ) .
Mechanical completion achieved Plant 6 in FY26 and we are on track for commercialization by H1 FY27, Integrate Plant-6 into digital planning and QMS systems from day one Deploy Plant-6 capacity across all three verticals to reduce lead times and improve customer responsiveness

The Company has built expertise in multi-drug formulations, fixed dosage combinations (FDCs), and modified-release formats - capabilities increasingly valued by pharmaceutical partners

In FY26, R&D expenditure grew to 83 Millions from 63 Millions in FY 25 ( 24% increase )

items.title: Leverage in South Korean stocks is out of control: Assets under management (AUM) in South Korea's leveraged ETFs are up to a record ~$45 billion. AUM ...
items.description_html:
Leverage in South Korean stocks is out of control:

Assets under management (AUM) in South Korea's leveraged ETFs are up to a record ~$45 billion.

AUM has surged +800% since the start of 2026.

As a result, leveraged exposure as a % of free float market capitalization, the portion of shares actually available for public trading, is up to a record ~2.9%, more than TRIPLING since January.

Meanwhile, the Hong Kong-listed 2x leveraged long SK Hynix ETF rose to ~$15 billion in assets at its peak, the largest single-stock leveraged product in the world.

By comparison, four leading 2x leveraged long ETFs tracking Micron, $MU, Nvidia, $NVDA, Sandisk, $SNDK, and Tesla, $TSLA, have each never exceeded $10 billion in assets.

Leverage in Korea is at extreme levels.

items.title: I finally have an announcement to make! I've been creating content for almost a decade, and so many of you have told me my work has impacted your inve...
items.description_html:
I finally have an announcement to make!

I've been creating content for almost a decade, and so many of you have told me my work has impacted your investing in some way. That means everything to me!

So a little over a year ago I decided to formalize things and got SEBI-registered. For a finance content creator already doing well, you might ask why add all that compliance and complexity. Simple, I felt quality stock market research that is also accountable was missing.

A lot of you in my circle have heard me mention an app I've been building. Since then I've built a team of around 20, mostly in research (fundamental and technical), plus tech, customer success, and operations.

Together, we built @RecoWealth, a platform and community for Quality Equity Research, Curated Stock Recommendations with Transparency, and a genuine community around all things investing in India and US.

We think most people are underexposed to equity in general, and even more so to global markets.

We didn't want to be just another place for stock tips so we've vertically integrated to be with you from the start of your investing journey and as a partner throughout.

Time to change the way people look at Research! We are coming soon with our App! Stay Tuned.....

items.title: RT Moses Harding John: Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of th...
items.description_html:
RT Moses Harding John
Now that I am seeing the Life & General Insurance businesses closely, I notice that distributors walk away with big chunk of the cake while the product manufacturers struggle to “contain” claim settlements (adjusted for reinsurance) & operating costs to stay in profit after adjusting the losses from core business against income from investment portfolio!

Cost of acquisition (distribution fees) + reinsurance + claim settlements + operating cost is always >100% net premium (gross premium adjusted for distribution fees + reinsurance) which is a funny business model! The profitability is high dependent on the performance of the investment portfolio, like an AMC!

Banks owning LI & GI (that includes health) businesses subsidize the cost of acquisition through their branch network to stay ahead in the race!

IRDAI needs to set up a fair play between the companies & distributors to make the insurance sector thrive & be customer efficient!

Coverage of risks around life & health and movable & immovable assets is as critical as banking & financial services sector, but unfortunately not provided the desired regulatory attention beyond governance & compliance!

Insurance sector is like a (life & non-life) risk-underwriting AMC!


Ira Dugal: The planned overhaul of insurance commission is set to be wide -- from staggered commissions over the lifecycle of the product to a dynamic pricing model based on effort involved in servicing the policy.

Much needed reforms with deep implications.

Reuters @AshwinM_ reports.

items.title: Product mix changes often lead to "Re-rating" This has played out across many stocks over the years & remains one of the key catalysts to always watch...
items.description_html:
Product mix changes often lead to "Re-rating"

This has played out across many stocks over the years & remains one of the key catalysts to always watch out for.

Few examples where this has played out:

- Commodity companies transitioning towards higher value added products

Eg: Garware Hi Tech Films increasing its mix towards value added specialty films, steel tube players expanding their VAP portfolio, building material companies moving from commodity products to branded products.

- Many auto ancillary companies are making strategic acquisitions, entering newer categories & increasing content per vehicle.

- Retailers are shifting to private labels & higher margin offerings as part of their premiumisation strategy.

Eg: Jewellery companies increasing studded share & value retail players increasing private label contribution.

- Capital market companies diversifying REV streams

Brokers & capital market platforms are scaling wealth management, distribution and advisory businesses. Higher share of recurring income leads to lower cyclicality in the earnings trajectory.

- Hospitals witness higher EBITDA growth when occupancy improves & the mix shifts towards higher value specialities.

- Chemical companies moving up the value chain witness re-rating when they transition from commodity chemicals to speciality chemicals.

Many companies across sectors are trying to increase the share of higher margin value added businesses.

This is why product mix change remains one of the most important re-rating triggers.

items.title: At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The ...
items.description_html:
At anchor in the Persian Gulf, Abhijit Chopra found out about the US-Iran peace deal when his phone lit up with messages from family and friends. The captain of a crude oil tanker, he had to temper his excitement. There were no signs of celebration from nearby vessels, and no ships making haste towards the Strait of Hormuz.

Chopra and his 21-strong crew have been trapped since the war began in late February. At the beginning, they struggled with fear and uncertainty, which ebbed into boredom and a constant battle not to let negative thoughts set in. For more than 120 days they have waited, dining together and bonding by singing old Hindi songs at karaoke.

Full story
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-03/strait-of-hormuz-sailors-remain-trapped-despite-iran-us-ceasefire

items.title: Power equipment stocks are under pressure today The trigger is that the Ministry of Finance has granted a 2 year exemption from public procurement res...
items.description_html:
Power equipment stocks are under pressure today

The trigger is that the Ministry of Finance has granted a 2 year exemption from public procurement restrictions for 4 Chinese companies supplying power equipment to public sector firms in India

This has created concern for domestic power equipment manufacturers

Over the last few years, many power equipment companies had witnessed a massive re-rating. Strong order inflows, grid capex, transmission expansion, renewable integration etc helped the sector command premium valuations

In many cases, these stocks were already trading at very expensive multiples (Triple digit P/E)

Margins were also consistently expanding, which further strengthened the market narrative & pushed valuations higher

So, in a way, valuations were already very stretched & the sector needed some trigger to prick the optimism

The concern is that if Chinese companies are allowed to supply power equipment to public sector firms, domestic players may face higher competition

This can potentially lead to pricing pressure in tenders & can impact the margins

India will continue to need large investments in transmission, distribution, substations, transformers & grid modernization

But when stocks are trading at absurd valuations, even one negative surprise can lead to a big correction

items.title: BSE JULY Volumes HIT so far post RBI circular - Equirius Note BSE volumes down 37% Against the first week of June'26 Post-RBI circular volume check (e...
items.description_html:
BSE JULY Volumes HIT so far post RBI circular - Equirius Note

BSE volumes down 37% Against the first week of June'26

Post-RBI circular volume check (effective 1-Jul'26): 1st two trading sessions on BSE

We reviewed BSE's initial volume trends following the RBI circular that took effect on 1st July 2026.

# 0DTE (expiry day) — 2nd July'26: Premium turnover came in at Rs 547.02bn, ~19% below the 1QFY27 average expiry-day turnover (Rs 679.18bn) and about 11% below the June'26 average (Rs 617.29bn). Against the 1st week of June'26 (Rs 716.80bn), it is ~24% lower.

# 1DTE (one day to expiry) — 1st July'26: Turnover stood at Rs 355.11bn, ~9% below the 1QFY27 average (Rs 389.49bn) and about 11% below the June'26 average (Rs 397.25bn). Against the first week of June'26 (Rs 561.28bn), it is ~37% lower.

While it is early trends, however, Jul'26 volumes have started on a much lower number as compared to Jun'26, thus posing risks for downward revision to estimates in premium ADTO.

items.title: RT Press Trust of India: VIDEO | A remarkable piece of heritage has been restored in Karnataka's Belagavi district. A 300-year-old stepwell, which had...
items.description_html:
RT Press Trust of India
VIDEO | A remarkable piece of heritage has been restored in Karnataka's Belagavi district. A 300-year-old stepwell, which had been buried under garbage and debris, has been brought back to life.

The 80-feet-deep stepwell, believed to have been built during the Adil Shahi dynasty between the 15th and 17th century, has now been fully restored by the Belagavi-based Pyaas Foundation with the support of NSS volunteers and local residents. It reveals 53 stone steps, and a unique layout that looks like a Shiva Lingam when viewed from above.

#Belagavi #KarnatakaNews

(Full video available on PTI Videos - http://ptivideos.com)

items.title: RT Maheshwer Peri: There’s a particular kind of fear that only a parent knows. The fear that comes watching your child choose a life you can’t quite...
items.description_html:
RT Maheshwer Peri
There’s a particular kind of fear that only a parent knows. The fear that comes watching your child choose a life you can’t quite protect them.

Vinayak is an engineer by education. He was in product, good at his job, well liked and adding value. Everything about it looked right from where I stood: Security, salary, work place etc.

But he felt that he didn’t fit. Not as a complaint but a quiet truth. He didn’t like the closed spaces, the stillness and the daily routine.

On a family holiday, he took to deep sea diving. He went back again, to the same diving site. He learnt. He trained. And he started interning with them and helping tourists. He would check the oxygen cylinders, give demos, instructions. And he soon got certification as a deep sea diving instructor.

And one fine day, he announced that he is quitting from his corporate life. He went abroad and learnt more. And more. And more. He trained. Got every certification - On diving, surviving etc.

He is now a deep sea diving instructor, working in Indonesia. He earls less than half the pay, carries cylinders, spends long hours underwater, lives without the comforts I spent a career trying to provide. No AC room. Not even a wash basin in his room. No safety net.

He is happy. Underwater, undercompensated, and, by every real measure, more himself than he ever was in that office.

My fear hasn’t fully gone. I don’t think it’s meant to. He didn’t need me to protect him from this choice. He needed me to trust that he knew himself better than I did. That’s the harder thing to learn, as a parent: sometimes love means stepping back and letting someone be honest, brave and trusted.

I am proud. He had the courage that I didn’t have. He gave up comforts and chose what his heart said. He trained. Learnt. He was happy to be a subordinate than be the promoters son. He chose the difficult path. The not so treaded path. He is happy. And I am proud.

Happy birthday Vinayak.

items.title: Levered ETFs have become one of the most powerful forces in US equity markets: Daily rebalancing activity in US equity leveraged ETFs is up to a recor...
items.description_html:
Levered ETFs have become one of the most powerful forces in US equity markets:

Daily rebalancing activity in US equity leveraged ETFs is up to a record $50 billion.

Leveraged ETFs must rebalance daily to stay at their target leverage, adding more exposure after markets rise and reducing it after markets fall.

This figure has more than QUADRUPLED since the start of 2026.

Rebalancing activity now accounts for a record 1.60% of S&P 500 futures volume, exceeding the 2020-2024 peaks by 200%.

To put it simply, leveraged ETFs have grown large enough that their daily rebalancing can now amplify market moves in both directions.

Leveraged ETFs are amplifying market volatility like never before.

items.description_html:
BREAKING: The Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR) fell -5.5 million barrels last week, to 326 million barrels, the lowest since May 1983.

This marks the 13th consecutive weekly decline, the longest streak since the 2021-2023 period, per Zerohedge.

Over this time period, US oil reserves in the SPR have fallen -89 million barrels.

This represents 51.7% of the planned 172 million barrels to be released under a relief program coordinated by the IEA to lower energy costs.

By comparison, the US added +69 million barrels of oil to the SPR between July 2023 and March 2026.

US oil reserves are being depleted at a historic pace.

items.title: Retail dip-buying is reaching historic extremes: Average daily equity purchases by retail investors during S&P 500 down days are running at nearly 3.5...
items.description_html:
Retail dip-buying is reaching historic extremes:

Average daily equity purchases by retail investors during S&P 500 down days are running at nearly 3.5 times the daily average seen since 2020, the highest on record.

This is +56% higher than during the meme stock mania in 2021.

Year-to-date, retail purchases on down days are 2.3 times larger than on up days.

Even during S&P 500 up days, retail investors purchased nearly 1.5 times the daily average seen since 2020, so far this year, and double the figure posted in 2025.

Individual investors have consistently purchased more equities on down days than on up days in each of the last 7 years.

Retail investors are eager to buy dips.

items.title: History points to a strong July for US stocks: Since 2005, the S&P 500 has recorded an average gain of +2.5% in July, more than 4 times the average in...
items.description_html:
History points to a strong July for US stocks:

Since 2005, the S&P 500 has recorded an average gain of +2.5% in July, more than 4 times the average increase seen in the other 11 months of the year.

The index has not declined in July for 11 consecutive years, the longest positive streak of any month over this period.

Meanwhile, the S&P 500 has posted 24 all-time highs so far in 2026.

This ranks among the top 20 strongest starts to a year for the index since World War II..

Historically, when the index has seen such a streak, it has rallied another +6% over the following 6 months, on average.

Seasonal patterns suggest continued bullish momentum this month.

items.title: US-hiring plans remain historically depressed: US-based employers announced plans to add 10,933 jobs in June, marking a -44% decline from 19,536 in Ma...
items.description_html:
US-hiring plans remain historically depressed:

US-based employers announced plans to add 10,933 jobs in June, marking a -44% decline from 19,536 in May.

Year-to-date, US hiring plans rose +10% YoY, to 91,405 workers.

This comes as technology sector hiring plans surged +167% YoY, to 14,761 workers.

However, excluding technology, hiring plans are down -1% YoY, to 76,644 workers.

The decline has been driven by entertainment and leisure hiring plans, which are down -57% YoY to 12,096 workers so far in 2026.

This also follows 5 consecutive years of declining hiring intentions, which fell -84% since 2020, to 508,000 for the full year 2025.

Hiring continues to weaken beneath the surface.

items.title: Picture this: It's 2005 in Hyderabad, the pharmaceutical capital of India. A former CTO who just watched his previous company get acquired for $550 mi...
items.description_html:
Picture this: It's 2005 in Hyderabad, the pharmaceutical capital of India. A former CTO who just watched his previous company get acquired for $550 million decides to bet everything on a radical idea—build a pharma company that treats African AIDS patients with the same quality standards as American cancer patients. No shortcuts. No "good enough for emerging markets." Just world-class chemistry at prices the developing world could afford.

That man was Dr. Satyanarayana Chava, and his company, Laurus Labs, would go on to become the world's largest third-party supplier of antiretroviral APIs, commanding 50% of the global market for key HIV drugs. Today, with a market cap north of ₹82,500 crore ($5.5 billion), Laurus has transformed from that single-minded API startup into an integrated pharmaceutical powerhouse spanning drug discovery to commercial manufacturing.

However, what makes this story remarkable is that, unlike the typical Indian pharma playbook of reverse-engineering expired patents and competing on price, Laurus built its empire on original chemistry.

Half of its 7000 employees work in R&D. The company holds over 300 patents. And in an industry where "Made in India" often meant a "cheaper alternative," Laurus supplies ingredients to nine of the ten largest generic pharmaceutical companies globally.

Src – empor top , no reco

items.title: RT David Sacks: Legacy Media types are calling this Alex Karp interview a “crash-out” so that’s your first clue that he is actually saying somethin...
items.description_html:
RT David Sacks
Legacy Media types are calling this Alex Karp interview a “crash-out” so that’s your first clue that he is actually saying something extremely insightful. He is articulating what real “AI safety” looks like in the enterprise.

Not abstract alignment research or certification by a government-run DMV for AI. Real AI safety for businesses is the ability to control their own data, model weights, and compute — so a frontier lab can’t hoover up their proprietary knowledge and turn it into their next product.

As Karp explains, technical customers want “control over their compute, their models, their data stack, and their alpha. They want to know they own the means of production, and it’s not being transferred to someone else.”

Don’t think that can happen? Just look at Figma. According to The Information, Anthropic “blindsided” its then-business partner with the launch of Claude Design. Figma’s founder said Anthropic had not been “consistently honest” with them. Anthropic’s chief product officer had even served on Figma’s board until three days before the launch of Claude Design. Figma’s stock has fallen sharply this year while Anthropic’s valuation has surged.

This isn’t an isolated example. Anthropic has launched Claude Science, Claude Security, Claude Legal, and of course Claude Code — each expanding into categories previously served by companies building on top of their models. The pattern is consistent: watch where value is being created, then move in directly. Dominate the model layer, then use that position to capture the most lucrative verticals.

Dario has argued that open source models powerful enough to compete with Anthropic are “dangerous.” But dangerous to whom? Not to enterprises that want to retain control over their data and workflows. Dangerous to a business model that benefits from customers having few real alternatives at the model layer.

As Karp exposes, true enterprise safety isn’t trusting that a lab’s future roadmap won’t include your business. It’s retaining the ability to choose — at the model layer — who gets to see and use your alpha.


Palantir: Palantir CEO Alex Karp on what customers actually want, the real business of frontier labs, and the importance of open source models:

“What the technical customers want is control over their compute, their models, their data stack, and their alpha. They want to know they own the

items.title: Bloomberg: Since Bretton Woods cemented the global dominance of the dollar in 1944, "Americans and the world have been arguing about it. Some US analy...
items.description_html:
Bloomberg: Since Bretton Woods cemented the global dominance of the dollar in 1944, "Americans and the world have been arguing about it. Some US analysts worry about losing that status; a few think it’s not worth all the trouble. Plenty of non-Americans resent the dollar’s dominance; no one can put forward a better alternative."

Count me among those who thinks US dollar dominance is definitely not worth the trouble, but who also believe that its dominance will remain as long as the US is wiling (and credibly able), for political reasons, to embrace an overvalued dollar that sacrifices the interest of its manufacturers, workers and middle classes for the benefit of Wall Street, the US foreign affairs establishment, and mercantilist economies that need to acquire foreign assets to balance their trade surpluses.
https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-07-02/can-the-us-dollar-remain-the-world-s-reserve-currency?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=twitter

items.title: RT Intrinsic Compounding: On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM...
items.description_html:
RT Intrinsic Compounding
On 30th June 2026, the Expenditure Finance Committee cleared a Rs 1.25 trillion outlay for ISM 2.0, against Rs 760bn for ISM 1.0. Union Cabinet approval is expected shortly. But the number is the smaller part of the story.

ISM 1.0 was a manufacturing-enablement scheme: get plants built, prove India can execute at scale and quality, put a 50% central capex subsidy behind fabs, OSAT and display units, disbursed pari-passu with capital deployment rather than tied to production targets like a PLI scheme.

That single design choice, government co-investing from day one instead of paying out after commissioning, is what let India move from approval to construction faster than most Western programmes.

And now ISM 2.0 is altogether a different story where it adds three new pillars on top of the manufacturing base: equipment and materials, IP and deeptech development, and talent at scale.

ECMS, the components and materials scheme, has been doubled to Rs 400bn. Basic customs duty has been removed on 25 critical minerals, lithium, cobalt, germanium among them, the first real move toward an indigenous materials base. On IP, the scheme is now explicitly targeting full-stack Indian chip design as a stated goal, DPIIT recognition for semiconductor startups has been extended to 20 years, and a datacentre tax holiday runs to 2047. On talent, the target is 85,000 industry-ready engineers by CY27, a workforce of process engineers, metrology specialists and cleanroom technicians that India's 3 lakh chip designers, excellent for GCC-style design work, simply cannot supply on their own. In short: ISM 1.0 proved India could build the box. ISM 2.0 is trying to make sure India doesn't have to import everything that goes inside it.

ISM was never trying to win at the leading edge; sub-7nm logic is absent from the roadmap and will stay absent for a long time. Instead it targets 28-110nm logic and OSAT, segments that together account for roughly 56% of global wafer capacity and cover almost everything India's own auto, industrial and consumer electronics actually consume.

India's cost base in OSAT runs an estimated ~45% below Taiwan on an indexed basis and ~10% below Malaysia, already one of the most competitive geographies globally.

Combine that with a design workforce that is roughly a fifth of the world's chip-design talent pool, second only to the US, and you get a genuinely differentiated entry point: cheap assembly, deep design talent, and a captive domestic demand base of $62bn today headed to $155bn by CY31 as electronics production scales toward the government's $500bn FY31 target.

Across design, India's presence is really two layers :- the GCC arm of global majors doing serious front-end work (Intel's ~$9bn design centre, Qualcomm's team that did the Feb'26 2nm tape-out, AMD's largest global design centre in Bengaluru, plus NXP, Nvidia, Marvell, Renesas, Texas Instruments) sitting alongside an early but genuine domestic fabless layer (Aura Semiconductor in analog/mixed-signal, Mindgrove in RISC-V SoCs, Saankhya Labs in SDR/5G broadcast backed by Tata's Tejas Networks, InCore Semiconductors spun off IIT Madras's SHAKTI processor programme, Silizium Circuits and Agnit in GaN, iVP Semiconductor in power discretes, Netrasemi in edge-AI chiplets);

In wafer fabrication India has exactly one project, the Tata Electronics-PSMC fab at Dholera targeting 28-110nm and first chip in Dec'26; OSAT/ATMP is where the actual manufacturing muscle is concentrated Micron's Sanand ATMP (DRAM/NAND, operational), Kaynes Semicon at Sanand (IPMs for EVs, operational), CG Power's CG Semi at Sanand (automotive/industrial packages, G1 live, G2 by end-CY26), Tata Electronics' Jagiroad OSAT in Assam (largest single OSAT bet, phase-1 commissioning), the HCL Group-Foxconn JV at Jewar (targeting CY28), plus a newer batch approved in Aug'25/May'26, 3D Glass Solutions and SiCSem in Odisha, Continental Device in Punjab, Advanced System in Package in Andhra Pradesh, Crystal Matrix and Suchi Semicon in Gujarat;

And upstream in equipment/materials, still the weakest layer, you've got Linde India, INOX Air Products and INOX India building out gas infrastructure at Dholera, Navin Fluorine and Gujarat Fluorochemicals moving into electronic-grade acids, Deepak Nitrite/Deepak Fertilisers into electronic-grade IPA, Aether Industries and Tatva Chintan and Jubilant Ingrevia into specialty semicon chemicals, Thermax/VA Tech Wabag/Praj Industries in ultrapure water systems, Carborundum Universal in fab-equipment ceramics, Voltas in cleanroom HVAC (already executing for Tata Electronics), and Siemens supplying automation into fab and OSAT plants.

Nearly all of this last group is still at the trial-order or MoU stage rather than committed fab contracts, which is precisely the gap ISM 2.0's new equipment-and-materials pillar is trying to force closed.

But this can be a interesting turn to Indian Semicon space in general

Across design, India's presence is really two layers :- the GCC arm of global majors doing serious front-end work (Intel's ~$9bn design centre, Qualcomm's team that did the Feb'26 2nm tape-out, AMD's largest global design centre in Bengaluru, plus NXP, Nvidia, Marvell, Renesas, Texas Instruments) sitting alongside an early but genuine domestic fabless layer (Aura Semiconductor in analog/mixed-signal, Mindgrove in RISC-V SoCs, Saankhya Labs in SDR/5G broadcast backed by Tata's Tejas Networks, InCore Semiconductors spun off IIT Madras's SHAKTI processor programme, Silizium Circuits and Agnit in GaN, iVP Semiconductor in power discretes, Netrasemi in edge-AI chiplets);

In wafer fabrication India has exactly one project, the Tata Electronics-PSMC fab at Dholera targeting 28-110nm and first chip in Dec'26; OSAT/ATMP is where the actual manufacturing muscle is concentrated Micron's Sanand ATMP (DRAM/NAND, operational), Kaynes Semicon at Sanand (IPMs for EVs, operational), CG Power's CG Semi at Sanand (automotive/industrial packages, G1 live, G2 by end-CY26), Tata Electronics' Jagiroad OSAT in Assam (largest single OSAT bet, phase-1 commissioning), the HCL Group-Foxconn JV at Jewar (targeting CY28), plus a newer batch approved in Aug'25/May'26, 3D Glass Solutions and SiCSem in Odisha, Continental Device in Punjab, Advanced System in Package in Andhra Pradesh, Crystal Matrix and Suchi Semicon in Gujarat;

And upstream in equipment/materials, still the weakest layer, you've got Linde India, INOX Air Products and INOX India building out gas infrastructure at Dholera, Navin Fluorine and Gujarat Fluorochemicals moving into electronic-grade acids, Deepak Nitrite/Deepak Fertilisers into electronic-grade IPA, Aether Industries and Tatva Chintan and Jubilant Ingrevia into specialty semicon chemicals, Thermax/VA Tech Wabag/Praj Industries in ultrapure water systems, Carborundum Universal in fab-equipment ceramics, Voltas in cleanroom HVAC (already executing for Tata Electronics), and Siemens supplying automation into fab and OSAT plants.

Nearly all of this last group is still at the trial-order or MoU stage rather than committed fab contracts, which is precisely the gap ISM 2.0's new equipment-and-materials pillar is trying to force closed.

But this can be a interesting turn to Indian Semicon space in general

The Trump Administration may soon have equity in US AI giants.

items.title: You will very soon reach a point of saturation reading investment related books , as broadly you will become aware of few things , that's when you mus...
items.description_html:
You will very soon reach a point of saturation reading investment related books , as broadly you will become aware of few things , that's when you must read more Financial History ,Authors like Taleb , Peter Theil , Annie duke , which tell you to think with asymmetric information to outcomes , and how to think more than what to think

Probabilistic nature of outcomes , survival through optionality , role of prepared luck and being antifragile Must read all this

Read history , globally successful business models ( founders podcast is a great idea)

I have tried to do this , investment books after a point is the same thing , I am picking up annual reports of the iconic global business models that have done well

1) Mckesson Corp
2) Airbus
3) Costco
4) Reading about newer markets , visualUsing Indian companies in a global context
4) Global Big pharma annual reports

Broaden reading that helps unlock optionality , that is what I have been working on as well

items.title: IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday ...
items.description_html:
IT is up. Everyone is looking for a news or reason. Maybe it's just the realization that pessmism is running ahead of reality (tweeted this yesterday also).

Allied reading:
https://www.ndtvprofit.com/technology/palantir-ceo-karp-slams-anthropic-openai-token-pricing-something-has-gone-completely-wrong-11715871
Strong remarks made by Palantir CEO Alex Karp yesterday. criticized the token-based pricing approach used by AI labs like Anthropic and OpenAI, arguing that “something has gone completely wrong.” He said rising AI costs and newer, pricier model generations are frustrating enterprise customers, leading them to avoid “tokenmaxxing” (spending primarily based on tokens) and instead focus on clearer return on investment.

Karp suggested enterprises may shift toward open-weight models that can deliver similar capabilities at lower cost, and he highlighted concerns that China is advancing rapidly in AI development. He also emphasized a trend away from broad, general-purpose AI and toward more efficient, proprietary tools tailored to specific businesses.

In line with Palantir’s broader stance, Karp positioned open-weight approaches (including Palantir’s work with Nvidia) as a solution—framing the key need for buyers as control over compute, models, and data (the “means of production”), rather than handing that control to external providers.

items.title: "China was at India’s 2025 per capita GDP in 2007 and ranked 123. It has moved up to 73 and is on the verge of high-income status with a per capita i...
items.description_html:
"China was at India’s 2025 per capita GDP in 2007 and ranked 123. It has moved up to 73 and is on the verge of high-income status with a per capita income nearly five times that of India.

Vietnam was at our level in 2016 and is now ranked 122, with per capita GDP around 60% higher than India’s.

Can India reach high-income status? And by when?"

Good read by @neelkanthmishra
https://tessellatum.in/?p=625

"India is trying to cover the economic distance from low-income to high-income in about one-third the time it took the currently developed nations, but demographics are changing five to eight times faster.

Fertility is falling more rapidly than expected, and median age of the population may cross 40 well before the current projection of 2053.

Growth has been seen to slow substantially for countries after their median age crossed 40: the demographic dividend becomes a demographic tax as dependency ratios (number of dependents per worker) rise, labour force shrinks, and growth in productivity and demand slows."

items.title: Chat with S. Naren of ICICI Pru AMC today. 5 points. Watch below. 1. "Clearly the macro is in much better shape.. but we still believe in moderate ret...
items.description_html:
Chat with S. Naren of ICICI Pru AMC today.

5 points. Watch below.

1. "Clearly the macro is in much better shape.. but we still believe in moderate returns.. still not in a period where you can give a thumping the table call on equity.. we have to still wait for the AI bucket to either start underperforming for India to get a big part of the FII flows"

2. "Midcaps are the most overvalued relative to large caps and small caps.. we are constructive on both large caps and on small caps.."

3. "You need the growth mindset and not defensive value mindset for you to play the 'electrification theme' (industrials/utlities etc)... these are not growing at 5-10% but much faster.. BUT if the global AI theme were to deflate, this would also deflate"

4. "Private sector banks have derated significantly that you should clearly expect decent returns from private sector banks now.."

5. "We are very positive on oil and gas sector”

#Nifty #BankNifty #Investing #Macros

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=W1RiA2ELYxo

items.description_html:
"In June, foreign investors continued to aggressively sell EM equities, with net outflows of US$57bn, bringing YTD to US$144bn, the highest in more than
a decade.

Outflows impacted almost every market (except small net inflows into Thailand), with especially strong outflows from Korea (US$30bn) and Taiwan (US$18bn), with outflows also accelerating for Indonesia and South Africa while the unwind of "Buy Brazil" trade continued.

The only good news was that the intensity of foreign selling in India decreased (US$3bn) and there were signs of some tentative return of China Southbound buyers."

- Viktor Shvets, Global Strategist, Macquarie

items.title: This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very hig...
items.description_html:
This version of story confuses several things. The Plaza Accord did not "break" Japan's development model. That model was already broken once very high investment rates no longer corresponded to the need for a massive rebuilding of infrastructure, housing stock and manufacturing capacity after the devastation of war.

Japan's growth model, which was the right growth model after the war when it was severely underinvested in nearly every sector, had made itself (through its very success) obsolete by the 1970s, as Albert Hirschman explained would happen, although, he warned, policymakers would find it extremely difficult to change the model, and so would likely go on to create a very different and opposite set of imbalances.

That is exactly what happened in Japan (and, indeed, in every country that followed the high-saving/high-investment development model), with the resulting overinvestment showing up clearly in the continued very-high investment share of GDP through the early 1990s (among the highest in history until China surpassed it in the mid 1990s) and, even more clearly, in the surge in the country's debt burden.

The latter is key, because when nearly all debt creation is used to fund investment, as was the case in Japan, the country's debt burden cannot rise if the investment is on average productive for the economy. The fact that it rose so rapidly for so long is strong evidence of massive investment misallocation.

That is why for years Tokyo had devalued its currency in real terms, which is just another way of saying that it forced up the value of the currencies of its trade partners. It did this to minimize the consequences of domestic overinvestment in manufacturing capacity (by exporting highly subsidized excess capacity) and to help mitigate the debt impact of its domestic overinvestment.

That is also why to say the problem was the "sudden" revaluation of the yen misses the point. For nearly two decades Washington (and policymakers in many other capitals) had asked Tokyo for a gradual revaluation of the yen (i.e. it asked Japan to stop forcing the dollar to be overvalued), but as Tokyo was unable to embrace any meaningful appreciation of the yen as long as it refused to take the necessary steps to rebalance its domestic economy, this left the US with only two options.

First, it could do nothing and suffer from the continued overvaluation of the dollar imposed on it by the Bank of Japan's currency policies. And second, because the US could not intervene directly in its currency the way Japan could, it could use the threat of tariffs (the standard alternative to currency devaluation) to force a near-term revaluation of the yen.

It chose the latter, but revaluation rebalances the domestic economy by transferring income from manufacturers (net exporters) to households (net importers) thereby raising the household share of GDP.

Tokyo, however, subsequently reversed this transfer by lowering interest rates and expanding investment lending, which transferred income back from households (net lenders to the banking system) to manufacturers (net borrowers).

That is almost certainly why the Plaza Accord were ultimately so much more painful for Japan than for Germany – not because the Plaza Accord created the problem, but rather because Germany never suffered from nearly the same extent of overinvestment as Japan, and began to adjust after the revaluation, whereas Tokyo tried to put off adjustment for several years longer by igniting an asset bubble and exacerbating the investment bubble.

Beijing is clearly determined to avoid the fate of Japan, but I worry that by misinterpreting what happened in Japan in the 1980s, it risks doing even worse than Japan did.


The China Academy: 1/7
Pettis and Setser are right that bad investment + debt matters. But this is a strawman. The Chinese Plaza lesson is not “one treaty cursed Japan.” It is: don’t let external pressure force a sudden currency move that breaks your developmental model.

items.title: RT VisionaryVoid: Nobody Could Explain Why They Were Hated. They Were Hated Anyway. For roughly 700 years, a group of people lived in southwestern Fra...
items.description_html:
RT VisionaryVoid
Nobody Could Explain Why They Were Hated. They Were Hated Anyway.

For roughly 700 years, a group of people lived in southwestern France and northern Spain called the Cagots. They spoke the same language as their neighbors, practiced the same Catholic faith, and were physically indistinguishable from the people around them. In 1880, a researcher who spent years studying the community concluded formally that he could find "no evidence which marks the Cagots out as a people distinct from the surrounding inhabitants." No different race, no different religion, no different language. Nothing.

They were still treated as untouchables. When they came to Mass, they entered through a separate side door, often designed to be intentionally low, forcing them to bow. They had their own holy water fonts; touching the shared one was forbidden. Priests served them communion at the end of long wooden spoons, to avoid skin contact. When they walked into a market town, they were required to shake a rattle to warn people of their approach, like a leper announcing himself. They wore a red badge in the shape of a goose's foot so no one would accidentally stand too close. They were banned from touching food in markets, from entering butchers and bakeries without announcement, from marrying outside their group. They were confined to ghettoes, called Cagoteries, usually built on the malarial side of the river. They were permitted to work as carpenters, masons, and rope-makers. In this capacity, they built many of the very churches that required them to enter through the servants' door. At some point in the 18th century, a wealthy Cagot in Brittany touched the normal holy water font. A soldier cut off his hand and nailed it to the church door.

Theories about their origin ran for centuries: descendants of Moorish soldiers, leftovers of the Visigoth invasions, heirs of Albigensian heretics, former lepers, members of a rival medieval woodworking guild whose enemies' contempt had hardened into law. None of them held up. The most recent serious theory, proposed in 2007 by British historian Graham Robb, is that they were simply a guild of skilled medieval craftsmen whose commercial rivals had successfully smeared them, and the smear ossified over five centuries into something that no one could any longer explain or undo.

The French Revolution abolished the legal distinction in 1789. Many Cagots raided local archives and destroyed every document that named them, erasing the paper trail of their own persecution. They assimilated and disappeared. Over 60 churches in the Pyrenees still have their separate Cagot entrances, bricked up now, partially obscured by paint and ivy. Nobody inside those churches ever really knew why they were built.

The most efficient prejudice is the kind that outlasts its own explanation.

items.title: RT Bull Theory: BREAKING: OpenAI has reportedly proposed giving a 5% equity stake to the U.S. government, according to the Financial Times. At OpenAI'...
items.description_html:
RT Bull Theory
BREAKING: OpenAI has reportedly proposed giving a 5% equity stake to the U.S. government, according to the Financial Times.

At OpenAI's current $852 billion valuation, a 5% stake would be worth $42.6 billion.

CEO Sam Altman has reportedly pitched the idea directly to President Trump, Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick and Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent as a way to align the public with AI's economic upside and reduce political resistance.

The proposal would encourage other leading U.S. AI companies - including Anthropic, Google and Meta to contribute similar stakes to a sovereign wealth-style fund modeled after the Alaska Permanent Fund, which pays dividends from state investment returns.

The discussions are preliminary and any arrangement would likely require approval from Congress.

items.description_html:
Coforge , some interesting highlights from the management in recent conference call that tell us a lot about the company as well and the sector too

1) They have defined and believe that 6 MOAT's will help the company actually benefit from the massive AI opportunity and have spelled them out really well ( do give it a read to also understand where the sector is tilting not only coforge )

2) Focusing on delivering 100% FCF/PAT conversion in FY27 , with good profitability growth H2 Heavy

3) They are expecting a flattish Q1 for numbers , there is a low-margin portfolio in India that we will discontinue immediately, and the negative impact of that will flow into Q1. Despite that significant portfolio, we expect to be flattish in Q1 and to be on a very fast growth trajectory from Q2 onwards ( again pointing to H2 heavy growth )

But what is interesting is downscaling low margin business is also brave in a sector that is not finding incremental deal wins , focusing on Encora Integration , pivoting to higher quality businesses are all steps in the right direction for terminal value

Also if you haven't already, do check out their investor day decks that they have detailed and key insights about their strategy going forward , see link below

https://investors.coforge.com/investor-day-2026

items.description_html:
Axis capital on Coal India
ADD rating with TP at ₹485
🚨 E-auction volume +45% YoY in Q1FY27, realization also improves 🚨

1️⃣ Coal dispatch growth in June at 7.5% YoY is the highest in the last 28 months. Q1FY27 dispatch +3.5% YoY.

items.title: Retail investors are stepping away from Magnificent 7 stocks: Retail investors accounted for just 6% of total Magnificent 7 trading volume over the 5 ...
items.description_html:
Retail investors are stepping away from Magnificent 7 stocks:

Retail investors accounted for just 6% of total Magnificent 7 trading volume over the 5 trading days ending Friday, the lowest in 4 years.

Individual investors showed less interest in Magnificent 7 stocks over the last week than on roughly 85% of trading days since 2022.

By comparison, retail trading accounted for more than 20% of Magnificent 7 volume during some periods in 2023 and 2024, and remained above 15% for most of 2025.

As a result, the 5-day moving average of retail trading volume in Magnificent 7 stocks is down to ~25 million shares, the lowest in at least 18 months.

This marks a -79% decline from the April 2025 peak of ~120 million shares.

Retail interest is shifting from the Magnificent 7 toward semiconductor stocks.

items.description_html:
BREAKING: Total crypto card deposits have officially crossed above $10 billion for the first time in history.

This puts total deposits up +82% year-to-date and nearly +250% year-over-year.

The surge comes amid a rapid adoption of stablecoins as a payment rail, particularly in cross-border transactions.

Yesterday, Visa, Mastercard, and a consortium of over 140 companies jointly launched a new USD-pegged stablecoin called Open USD, which is set to accelerate this trend.

Onchain platform Jupiter Mobile has also driven adoption, which has seen +65% month-over-month growth in new card users.

User growth has been fueled by localized integrations such as QR-based payments and growing accessibility across 60+ countries.

Crypto card adoption is rapidly expanding.

items.title: US job openings are rebounding: US job openings jumped +9,000 in May, to 7.6 million, the highest since May 2024. This follows a +698,000 surge in Apr...
items.description_html:
US job openings are rebounding:

US job openings jumped +9,000 in May, to 7.6 million, the highest since May 2024.

This follows a +698,000 surge in April, the biggest monthly addition since September 2022.

At the same time, total private job openings rose +2,000, to 6.8 million, the highest since March 2024, following a +635,000 increase in April.

As a result, the ratio of job openings to unemployed workers is up to 1.0, the highest since January 2025.

There are now 287,000 more available vacancies than unemployed workers, the largest difference since January 2025.

Is AI creating new jobs?

items.title: RT Alireza Talakoubnejad: Interesting excerpt from Ghalibaf's (Speaker of Parliament and head of the Islamic Republic's negotiating team) interview wi...
items.description_html:
RT Alireza Talakoubnejad
Interesting excerpt from Ghalibaf's (Speaker of Parliament and head of the Islamic Republic's negotiating team) interview with State TV in which he says that the Strait of Hormoz is valuable to Iran when there is *increasing* traffic in it.

He points out the Strait didn't have the geopolitical importance it did today 100 years ago when there was no oil industry moving through it (implying scaring away traffic will reduce Iran's leverage) and that it isn't in Iran's interest to make a situation where in the long run all countries come up with alternate routes.

As such it is very much in Iran's interest to make the Strait (and Iranian side of it) as appealing as possible & increase traffic.

This interview comes as there is an increase in vocal disagreement in Tehran between pro-deal factions led by Ghalibaf & Pezeshkian and opponents of the agreement.

items.title: 🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE. Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may...
items.description_html:
🚨🚨🚨 We have the first Persian Gulf country lifting its oil exports to (if not even above) pre-war levels: UAE.

Sure, the 'ketchup effect' may be inflating exports a bit (from inventories). But whatever that contribution is, clearly June oil exports have surged to >95% of pre-war levels (and some tanker trackers have UAE June oil exports even above pre-war levels).

With exports surging, the recovery in oil wellhead production won't be far behind, me thinks.

Importantly, UAE oil is flowing well beyond its typical trade partners. With Chinese demand down, Abu Dhabi is shipping lots of crude into Europe and the US.

items.title: There's a reason why D2C brands are growing so rapidly in India. Walk into many urban households today, and you'll notice the shift: • Floor cleaners...
items.description_html:
There's a reason why D2C brands are growing so rapidly in India.

Walk into many urban households today, and you'll notice the shift:

• Floor cleaners & home care: Legacy brands → Koparo
• Shampoo & personal care: Legacy brands → Plum
• Protein: Avvatar, PLIX, The Whole Truth
• Nutrition: Protein bars from Stroom, The Whole Truth & others
• Snacks: Protein & baked chips replacing traditional chips
• Instant meals: Health-focused noodles replacing conventional instant noodles

I feel these brands have two big advantages:

Thoughtful positioning - Non-toxic, plant-friendly home care (in Protein/fibre-rich foods - No palm oil, cleaner labels and better ingredients)

Quick commerce - Distribution is no longer a bottleneck. With 10–20 minute delivery, these brands now have mass reach and are just a tap away.

Indian consumers are increasingly paying for health, clean ingredients, and convenience—not just legacy brand names.

India's FMCG landscape is quietly undergoing a generational shift.

items.title: What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (bi...
items.description_html:
What Chamath is saying below is not wrong, but it is nothing new - maybe 2 years late, and doesn't add to the discourse. Yes, the old model of T&M (billing by the hour) is ending and shifting towards outcomes based pricing. The cost of new code generation is down by 80%, but it comes at its own cost (resources and community cost).

If the Enterprise world were to be built completely from scratch today, the "AI disrupts everything" argument would be true. As things stand today, there is a massive delta between how exponentially fast AI models are improving and how fast legacy multi-billion-dollar enterprises can meaningfully absorb them. This is creating a massive Enterprise deployment gap (refer image below from Infosys AI Investor day). This gap is a $300-400 Billion opportunity for Technology Services companies - albeit not IT players would benefit from this. There should be good value for IT players who can deliver the conversion layer with an AI-ready approach.

So far, the ROI for companies using AI instead of humans is not there and this may change in another year if the AI capex does not slow down. But as investors, at some point, we must ask - is the pessimism on IT running ahead of Enterprise reality?


Chamath Palihapitiya: Show me the incentive and I’ll show you the outcome.

The business model of Systems Integrators is to bill by the hour. You should not be surprised, then, when your project takes three years or more and is never finished.

An 8090 Software Factory project that finishes in three

Bengal's jute sector is witnessing a broader revival. Three more closed jute mills—Baranagar Jute Mill, Jagaddal Jute Industries and EMCO Jute Mill—are set to reopen, restoring jobs for over 7,000 workers. This follows the reopening of Naihati and Kankinara jute mills, which together are expected to bring back around 5,500 jobs, taking the total employment restoration to more than 12,500 workers across five mills.

Government of West Bengal has set a July 10 target to reopen all currently closed jute mills. If achieved, it would mark one of the largest coordinated revivals of Bengal's traditional jute industry in recent years, bringing thousands of workers back to production and signalling renewed momentum for a sector that remains central to the state's industrial economy.

items.title: Hero MotoCorp’s bet on Ather Energy may be one of the best examples of a corporate investment in an Indian startup. Hero has already made nearly 8x o...
items.description_html:
Hero MotoCorp’s bet on Ather Energy may be one of the best examples of a corporate investment in an Indian startup.

Hero has already made nearly 8x on its cumulative investment of about Rs 1,700 crore.

Its 30% stake is now worth roughly Rs 13,000 crore.

Ather Energy's market capitalisation touched a new high of Rs 44,000 crore today, nearly four times its valuation at last year's IPO.

Back in 2017, Hero first invested Rs 200 crore for a 28% stake in Ather, then a four-year-old startup valued at just Rs 635 crore at that time.

Hero continued backing the company through multiple funding rounds and the IPO, and is now a co-promoter alongside founders Tarun Mehta and Swapnil Jain - who together own about a 10% stake.

items.description_html:
RT Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port, Kolkata
Transforming Kolkata’s Riverfront: Blending Modern Infrastructure with Heritage

On 27.06.2026, Syama Prasad Mookerjee Port, Kolkata (SMPK) had the privilege of hosting an interactive session with Shri Sanjeev Sanyal
Member, Economic Advisory Council to the Prime Minister (EAC-PM).

The discussion centered on a transformative vision for the Hooghly Riverfront—stretching from Maa er Ghat (Bagbazar) to Millennium Park Ghat. The focus is to create a vibrant, people-centric public space while ensuring sustainable urban development and seamless city connectivity.

💡 A Vision of Living Heritage:
During the session, Shri Sanyal strongly advocated for upgrading the quality of urban life while strictly preserving the old essence of both sides of the Hooghly. He emphasized that iconic elements of Kolkata’s cultural fabric—such as the historic Mullick Ghat Flower Market and the traditional Kushti Akhras (wrestling arenas)—should be modernized and integrated, rather than replaced.
The high-level session was attended by:

Shri Rathendra Raman, Chairperson, SMPK

Shri Samrat Rahi, Deputy Chairperson, SMPK

Shri Devidatta Satpathy, Chief Vigilance Officer, SMPK

Senior Port Officials, distinguished urban experts, and representatives from The Bengal Chamber of Commerce and Industry (BCC&I).

Together, SMPK and its partners are committed to shaping a greener, more accessible, and culturally rooted waterfront for the City of Joy.

@shipmin_india
@PIB_ShipMin
@PIB_India

items.title: A very good Adam Tooze explanation of the current state of debate on Chinese imbalances. He is right, by the way, when he dismisses sectoral explanati...
items.description_html:
A very good Adam Tooze explanation of the current state of debate on Chinese imbalances. He is right, by the way, when he dismisses sectoral explanations (I call them “incremental” explanations) for the surge in overall imbalances by noting that “all macroeconomic dynamics, insofar as they involve any element of structural, “qualitative” change, are by definition, sectoral, industrial, or even corporate stories.”

To put it another way, you cannot explain a rise in China’s domestic macroeconomic imbalances (e.g. a rise in net exports) by pointing to a rise in a particular export (e.g. solar panels).

In a balanced economy, after all, more successful sales by solar-panel producers should result in rising employment, wages or investment that would, in turn, result in more imports by Chinese households and businesses.

The point is that in a well-functioning, balanced economy, in which wage growth is able to keep pace with productivity growth, a boost in exports of a particular sector or industry, or to a particular foreign client base, would not cause an equivalent boost in net exports but rather an equivalent boost in imports. What may look on the surface like a sectoral story (foreigners are buying more Chinese-made solar panels) is in fact a domestic imbalance story (rising production isn’t translating into rising wages and, thus, rising consumption).

This, as Tooze notes, has implication for EU policy. If China cannot or will not change domestic imbalances, the EU has two options. It can either do nothing, “But doing nothing is potentially destabilizing as well, Tooze writes. “Spectacular and uncontrolled deindustrialization must count as a serious economic, social and political risk in Europe.”

Or it can intervene in its external accounts to reverse the impact of Chinese intervention, although, as Tooze notes, “if trade protection and international industrial bargaining are indeed the best solution, then that risks compounding the disorder in world trade hitherto personified by Donald Trump.”

My only disagreement, of course, if that I would argue that the “disorder in world trade” occurred many decades before Donald Trump took the presidency. This is essentially what Joan Robinson warned must happen in her 1937 essay. Deficit economies will only accept beggar-thy-neighbor trade policies, she said, until the cost becomes too high, after which they will retaliate by themselves intervening in their external accounts.
@adam_tooze https://open.substack.com/pub/adamtooze/p/chartbook-454-china-shock-20-and?r=12ygf&utm_campaign=post-expanded-share&utm_medium=web

items.title: The Sudarshan Chakra is India's planned multi-layered air and missile defence shield, announced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his 2025 Independen...
items.description_html:
The Sudarshan Chakra is India's planned multi-layered air and missile defence shield, announced by Prime Minister Narendra Modi in his 2025 Independence Day address.

Named after Lord Krishna's divine discus, it aims to build a nationwide protective umbrella against aerial threats including drones, cruise missiles, ballistic missiles, and aircraft.

The government has set a target of making the system operational by 2035, drawing inspiration partly from Israel's Iron Dome but on a far larger scale suited to India's size and diverse threats along its borders with Pakistan and China.

The concept envisions an integrated network of sensors, command centres, and interceptors working together to detect, track, and neutralise incoming threats at multiple ranges and altitudes. It is meant to protect critical infrastructure, military installations, cities, and economic assets.

Defence equipment likely to be needed includes:

Long range surface to air missile systems such as the S-400 Triumf and the indigenous Project Kusha, designed to engage targets at extended ranges.

Medium and short range air defence systems like the Akash, Akash NG, and the Barak 8 (MRSAM), for layered coverage closer to protected assets.

Ballistic missile defence interceptors, including the two tier Prithvi Air Defence and Advanced Air Defence systems for exo and endo atmospheric interception.

Counter drone and counter UAS systems, including directed energy weapons (laser based systems) and electronic warfare jammers to tackle swarm drone threats.

Advanced radar and sensor networks, such as Swordfish long range tracking radars, AEW&C aircraft, and space based surveillance satellites for early detection.

Integrated command and control infrastructure to fuse data from all layers and coordinate interception in real time.

Very short range systems and point defence guns for terminal protection of high value targets.

Defence businesses in Radars, electronics, anti drone systems & those involved in indigenisation of SAM systems will see big traction over the next few years.

items.title: RT Michael Pettis: Setser is right, and I would add that those who think Japan's "lost decades" were caused by the Plaza Accord are engaged in some pr...
items.description_html:
RT Michael Pettis
Setser is right, and I would add that those who think Japan's "lost decades" were caused by the Plaza Accord are engaged in some pretty crude logical fallacies. After all the Plaza Accord didn't lead German to suffer the same "lost decades" as Japan, mainly because unlike Japan, Germany hadn't previously engaged in many years of unproductive investment (like Japan's famous "bridges to nowhere") and this unproductive investment hadn't been backed by a massive increase in the country's debt burden.

If you look at other countries that had investment-heavy growth models similar to that of Japan, most obviously the USSR and Brazil in the 1960s and early 1970s, they all suffered from crises or "lost decades", even though none of them entered into major, Plaza-Accord-style currency appreciations.

I know few analysts bother looking at the history of such growth models, but they should. If they did, they'd see that history doesn't tell us that any country that does a Plaza-Accord-style appreciation suffers from lost decades of growth (Germany and France didn't).

It tells us instead that any country that engages in many years of non-productive investment to keep GDP growth higher than the real economy can productively deliver, and that runs up huge corresponding debt burdens, is likely to suffer from lost decades as the excess capacity is gradually written down and the hidden losses allocated.


Brad Setser: The first error is that it is an unreasonable ask from uncompetitive economies. That uncompetitiveness is a function in part of price, and China is the one actively intervening in the market to hold the yuan down. the settlement numbers should this clearly

2/

items.description_html:
RT Nimesh Shah
CLSA on Kotak Bk
O-P, TP Rs 440
Announced acquisition of Deutsche Bank (DB) India’s retail banking, private banking and wealth management business
This business comprised Rs290bn in loans, Rs160bn in deposits and Rs105bn in wealth AUM as of FY26
Purchase price for this business was Rs2.8bn plus the difference in assets and liabilities acquired as of the purchase date.
However, no detail about the profitability of the business was provided by co
Only comment made was it would be “ROE accretive”.

HSBC on Kotak BK
Buy, TP Rs 460
KMB announced acquisition of Deutsche Bank’s India retail banking portfolio for a consideration of INR2.8bn
Portfolio adds c5.6% of loans, c2.8% of deposits, & c1.7% CASA to KMB’s existing balance sheet as of FY26
This acquisition does not meaningfully change outlook on stock

Nomura on Kotak BK
Buy, TP Rs 460
Kotak to acquire DBAG's retail, private banking & wealth business in India
Disciplined pricing: close to book value pricing reflects DBAG’s soft profitability profile
Good secured asset book, but a comparatively weaker liability franchise
Capital impact manageable; management guides to RoE accretion
View this is a sensible, disciplined acquisition, but don't see RoE/earnings benefit as substantial or immediate; success hinges on integration execution & client retention

Jefferies on Kotak Bk
Buy, TP Rs 450
Kotak Bank will acquire Deutsche Bank's India retail bkg, private bkg, & WM business for Rs2.8bn.
To Kotak's loans/deposits/AUM, it adds 6%/3%/1%; takes 84bps of CET1.
Deal looks accretive with scope to cross-sell; it comes days after Kotak's CEO chose not to renew his term after Dec-26
Conversation with Jefferies European Bk analyst indicates this trx may be aimed to simplify DB's structure & focus on core markets
@CNBCTV18Live @_RituSingh

items.title: RT Alex Veremeyenko: i'm obsessed with what's happening in AI reforestation right now this Franco-Brazilian startup called MORFO took a patch of land ...
items.description_html:
RT Alex Veremeyenko
i'm obsessed with what's happening in AI reforestation right now

this Franco-Brazilian startup called MORFO took a patch of land in Brazil that was rock-hard and compacted from years of cattle farming. they replanted it using a single drone. months later the ground was covered in grass, bushes, and small trees. the land came back to life.

here's how the whole thing works.

1. drones scan the terrain with high-resolution cameras and sensors
2. AI analyzes the imagery alongside soil samples, moisture levels, slope, and surrounding vegetation
3. the system picks from a catalog of 300+ native species, deciding exactly which plants will thrive in which specific spot
4. the drone fires biodegradable seed pods packed with seeds, nutrients, and moisture at 180 capsules per minute
5. satellite and drone imagery monitors regrowth over time, with AI tracking vegetation cover and biodiversity
6. two people and one drone cover 50 hectares a day. a person planting by hand manages about one hectare.

and MORFO isn't alone. AirSeed in Australia drops 250,000 seed pods per day into bushfire-scarred koala habitat, replanting swamp mahogany that koalas depend on to survive. Flash Forest in Canada fires 50,000 pods daily into wildfire-destroyed boreal forest, planning the replanting alongside Cree Indigenous communities. re-green won Prince William's Earthshot Prize after planting 6 million seedlings across 30,000 hectares of Amazon and Atlantic Forest.

five companies across four continents built this same approach independently. nobody coordinated. the physics of the problem demanded it.

knowing which seeds belong in which soil used to require years of ecological fieldwork, manual planting crews, and budgets that made large-scale restoration nearly impossible. now two people with a drone and an AI model trained on local soil data can replant 50 hectares before lunch.

this is the AI work that'll still matter in 50 years.

items.title: The Snag At 10:45 in the morning, I was already in Bangalore. Not really. The plane hadn't moved. But in my head I was three meetings deep, downloadin...
items.description_html:
The Snag

At 10:45 in the morning, I was already in Bangalore.

Not really. The plane hadn't moved. But in my head I was three meetings deep, downloading one concall after another, treating the flight as nothing more than a useful window to get reading done. The journey was just a corridor between where I sat and where I had decided to be.

Then the pilot came on. Some technical snag.

I did what most of us do with inconvenient news. I ignored it. Snags get fixed. Schedules hold. IndiGo, of all airlines, the one obsessed with being on time, would surely not let ninety minutes vanish on a taxiway.

But the announcements kept coming. The plane, already rolling toward the runway, stopped. Then it turned back. Then it just sat there while engineers climbed aboard the same machine that was supposed to carry me through the sky. And somewhere at the back of my mind, years of watching aircraft investigation documentaries started whispering. That quiet little catalogue of small ignored faults that later became headlines.

Ninety minutes passed. And then something I still can't fully explain moved in me. Not fear exactly. More like conviction. My friend and I stood up, and I asked the air hostess, politely, almost apologetically, to let us off. I had no reason I could name. My gut had simply stopped negotiating. We had no check-in baggage, so they allowed it. We walked back to the bus and sat down.

Twenty minutes later, from outside the plane now, we watched the entire flight get cancelled.

The next one was in the evening. I went home.

What stayed with me was this.

I had built my whole morning on the quiet assumption that I was in charge of how it would go. The meetings, the reading, the arrival time, all of it rested on one silent belief. The plane will fly because I need it to. But the plane did not care what I needed. One fault I couldn't even see dissolved the entire shape of my day in a moment.

We confuse our plans with control. They are not the same thing. Planning is arranging the few things we can actually touch. Control is a story we tell ourselves about everything else. The transcripts and the calendar were mine. The engine was not. The weather was not. The thousand small things sitting between intention and arrival never were.

And here is the strange part. The moment I finally let go of the plan, stood up, walked off, and accepted home instead of Bangalore, the whole thing resolved on its own, almost as if it had only been waiting for me to loosen my grip.

That gut feeling deserves some credit too. We are not as in control as we like to think, but we are also not powerless. Somewhere between total mastery and total helplessness sits the one real thing we own, which is how we respond. I could not fix the plane. I could choose to listen to that quiet voice, get up, and walk away.

In the larger scheme of things, I am very small. A passenger, not a pilot, of most of my life. The honest move is not to pretend otherwise. It is to plan lightly, hold loosely, and trust the instinct that tells you when to walk back to the bus.

The meetings waited. The morning did not go as planned. And I reached home oddly grateful, reminded of the one thing no calendar can ever teach me. I was never the one flying the plane.

We are just tiny cogs in grander scheme of things. Always be grateful to beauty of life 🙂

Looking at the plane from the bus and realising how small we are in grander scheme of things

items.title: Oyo is required to "use all net cash proceeds" - including through the IPO - towards the prepayment of the term loan as a part of its credit agreement...
items.description_html:
Oyo is required to "use all net cash proceeds" - including through the IPO - towards the prepayment of the term loan as a part of its credit agreement.

Oyo has already paid out close to Rs 1,100 crore in debt servicing in nine months of FY26 - a number which keeps rising.


Madhav Chanchani: Oyo plans to use Rs 4,988 crore from the proposed Rs 6,650 crore IPO - about 75% - to repay its massive debt.

It still has term loans (TLB) of over Rs 7,000 crore.

Most of the debt dates back to $825 mn TLB in 2021, but remaining to its Motel 6 acquisition two years ago.

Most of Oyo's business now comes from US, followed by Europe, Middle East and SEA.

This is thanks to acquisitions like Motel 6 in US (2024) and Leisure Group in Europe (2019).

India now accounts for less than 12% of gross bookings, down from 30% a couple of years ago.


Madhav Chanchani: 🚨 Hospitality player Oyo's parent co has filed for a Rs 6,650 crore IPO.

This is Oyo's third filing for a public listing, after attempts at going public in 2021 and 2023.

Besides founder Ritesh Agarwal, Softbank - which owns 40% stake - also listed as promoter.

items.title: This framework wasn't built in a weekend. It is the product of decades of investing, countless mistakes, thousands of hours of reading, writing, think...
items.description_html:
This framework wasn't built in a weekend.

It is the product of decades of investing, countless mistakes, thousands of hours of reading, writing, thinking and refining my own mental models.

AI didn't invent the framework.

It helped me organise and express years of accumulated judgement into something practical, testable and continuously improvable.

I think of AI as infrastructure.

Like a road.

The road doesn't decide where you go.

The destination depends on who's driving, what vehicle they're driving, and the judgement they bring to every turn.

This framework is Version 5.0 today.

Hopefully, it will never be finished.

items.title: "In the wake of Gulf crisis, the import-linked cost of a 14.2 kg cylinder rose above ₹1,600, yet the household price was held near ₹900, and lower s...
items.description_html:
"In the wake of Gulf crisis, the import-linked cost of a 14.2 kg cylinder rose above ₹1,600, yet the household price was held near ₹900, and lower still for the poorest."

Amidst the headlines of last 2 years - tariffs, AI, wars - one may miss the significance of a (rare) synchronous monetary and fiscal policy in action with a goal to support domestic consumption. Those who extol the China model will never understand the pay-off that comes from protecting bottom of pyramid consumption.

items.title: AI is a commodity. The moat lies in how you deploy it: custom capabilities, proprietary data, and disciplined systems. The hype and noise are seen. Th...
items.description_html:
AI is a commodity. The moat lies in how you deploy it: custom capabilities, proprietary data, and disciplined systems. The hype and noise are seen. The rules, decision-making, and repeatable processes are unseen. When the valuation bubble bursts, the entire value chain from data centres to uncle and aunt will bleed. The consumers of AI will be antifragile. They will gain from the chaos.


Antifragile Thinking: I may be invested in some or all of the companies mentioned. This is not a recommendation in any form.

The list is not exhaustive and will evolve as more data is incorporated.

items.title: Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true ...
items.description_html:
Thought provoking talk on portfolio risk management. Mark Spitznagel of Universa Investments explains that dedicating 3% of a stock portfolio to true "crash insurance" (deep OTM puts) boosts long-term compound growth better than relying on traditional "safe havens" like gold, Treasuries, or currencies, which often act as "imposters" by failing to deliver during actual crashes.

March 2008 - March 2020 period: A portfolio of 96.7% S&P 500 + 3.3% Universa tail-risk insurance delivered 11.5% annualized CAGR, versus 7.9% for 100% S&P 500 - a +3.6 percentage point improvement.

The 3.3% sleeve kept the overall portfolio essentially unscathed in March 2020 (when the S&P 500 fell 12.4%), while Universa itself returned +3,612% that month (+4,144% YTD).

Note: This is not a general recommendation.


0xDipper: Mark Spitznagel - founder of Universa, the "black swan" fund Nassim Taleb advises - on the most counterintuitive idea in investing:

add a 3% sliver of crash insurance to a stock portfolio. give that insurance a 0% expected return - it makes nothing over time.

it still raises
